title: Modern Horror and 'It Comes at Night' Director Trey Edward Shults
format: Sean Fennessey and critic K. Austin Collins on why It Comes at Night plays as mood over scares, then Sean talks to director Trey Edward Shults about grief, family, and Terrence Malick.

segments.heading: It Comes at Night and the state of modern horror

films.title: It Comes at Night
films.note: A24 release about a family holed up in a cabin during an unnamed plague. Both agree it works more as claustrophobic mood than as a jump-scare horror movie, and that the marketing sells it as more traditional than it is.

films.title: Krisha
films.note: Shults's first feature, about an estranged relative returning for Thanksgiving. Collins: a horror movie made out of domestic drama, capped by a falling turkey.

films.title: The Witch
films.note: The other recent A24 horror it gets compared to; Collins likes It Comes at Night more but sees both as families sealed off from society, fighting off an outside force.

films.title: The Shining
films.note: Cited for paranoia in enclosed spaces.

films.title: The Thing
films.note: Carpenter's version; Collins loves it and reads It Comes at Night the same way: the monster matters less than how it reshapes the people trapped together.

films.title: There Will Be Blood
films.note: Named among the operatic movies Shults talked about.

films.title: Get Out
films.note: Collins wrote about it earlier that year; raised as part of an interesting moment for the genre.

films.title: Unfriended
films.note: Told entirely through computer screens; Collins admires the audacity of its form even where the ending doesn't land.

films.title: It Follows
films.note: Collins reads it as much about family and friend bonds, the missing father and unseen mother, as about the unstoppable force.

films.title: The Bye Bye Man
films.note: A bad horror movie Collins keeps thinking about anyway, his example of the genre still giving you something.

segments.heading: Trey Edward Shults on It Comes at Night

films.title: It Comes at Night
films.note: Shults calls it a personal horror movie; the opening scene came from what he said to his own dying father. Influences he names: books on genocide and Bruegel's painting The Triumph of Death.

films.title: Krisha
films.note: Made for roughly $30,000 over nine days with family and friends after a failed 2012 attempt and a well-received short that drew no investors.

films.title: There Will Be Blood
films.note: The film he watched most while making It Comes at Night, for its mood.

films.title: The Tree of Life
films.note: Shults interned on the birth-of-the-universe second-unit footage as a teenager.

films.title: Voyage of Time
films.note: The Malick project that footage eventually became.

films.title: Aliens
films.note: A childhood favorite he snuck as a videotape and watched on loop overnight.

films.title: The Terminator
films.note: Among the action movies his father raised him on.

films.title: Die Hard
films.note: Named alongside Aliens and Terminator as the action he loved as a kid.

films.title: A Clockwork Orange
films.note: One of the films that made him see movies differently as he got older.

films.title: Raging Bull
films.note: Blew his mind on first viewing; he kept rewatching it and it's stayed a favorite since.

films.title: Rocky
films.note: His reference point for what Raging Bull wasn't.

films.title: Boogie Nights
films.note: The flow he wants for his next project, kids in high school and a family over a year, moving like a piece of music.

films.title: Goodfellas
films.note: Named with Boogie Nights as that musical, propulsive flow.

films.title: Blue Valentine
films.note: One of the heartwarming movies he took his mother to.

films.title: Alien
films.note: His childhood love, but he says he'd never make an Alien movie even if asked.
